coredata.dc:title: 0D/2D CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite with high photocatalytic disinfection performance under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Qing
Zhang, Wei-De
Shi, Qingshan
Yu, Yu-Xiang
coredata.dc:description: The development of sustainable and efficient photocatalysts for water disinfection has always been the challenge. Herein, 0D CQDs are grown in situ onto 2D Bi7O9I3 microsheets successfully for the first time, constructing a series of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite catalysts for photocatalytic disinfection. Compared with pure Bi7O9I3, the optimal 2-CB exhibits the highest photocatalytic antibacterial (99.99%) and degradation activity, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Bi7O9I3. CQDs can be used as photosensitizers to enhance light absorption and electron acceptors to promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The leakage of K+ ions and the structural destruction of the cell membrane cause bacteria death. Free radical trapping experiment proves that h+ plays a critical role on bacteria inactivation. The possible photocatalytic disinfection mechanism is also proposed. This work sheds light on designing highly-efficient photocatalysts toward water disinfection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Bi7O9I3
Photocatalysis
Water disinfection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) Bi7O9I3 and (b)2-CB; (c) EDX spectrum and (d–i) SEM-EDX elemental mappings of 2-CB.
TEM images of (a) CQDs, (b) 2-CB and (c) high magnification TEM image of 2-CB.
FT-IR spectra of the obtained catalysts.
XPS spectra of the catalysts: (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) I 3 ​d, (e) C 1s.

(a) Photocatalytic inactivation efficiency against E. coli on the catalysts under visible light irradiation; (b) Apparent rate constants for photocatalytic inactivation against E. coli on the catalysts.

Fluorescence images of E. coli treated with the 2-CB composite for different time of photocatalytic reaction, subsequently stained with SYTO9 (green) and PI (red) for 10 ​min. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
SEM images of E. coli before (a) and after (b) photocatalytic reaction.
(a) UV–vis spectra of Bi7O9I3, 1-CB, 2-CB, and 3-CB, and (b) Tauc plots of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB; (c) VB XPS spectra and (d) energy level distributions of CB and VB of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB.
(a) Transient photocurrent and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB.
Antibacterial efficiency against E. coli on 2-CB composite with different scavengers under visible light.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for water disinfection on 2-CB under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: 0D/2D CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite with high photocatalytic disinfection performance under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou, 510641, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou 510641, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application, Guangdong Institute of Microbiology, Guangzhou, 510070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China
Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Microbiology Southern China, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Microbial Culture Collection and Application, Guangdong Institute of Microbiology, Guangzhou, 510070, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou, 510641, China.
Corresponding author. Guangdong Institute of Microbiology, Guangzhou, 510070, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of sustainable and efficient photocatalysts for water disinfection has always been the challenge. Herein, 0D CQDs are grown in situ onto 2D Bi7O9I3 microsheets successfully for the first time, constructing a series of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite catalysts for photocatalytic disinfection. Compared with pure Bi7O9I3, the optimal 2-CB exhibits the highest photocatalytic antibacterial (99.99%) and degradation activity, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Bi7O9I3. CQDs can be used as photosensitizers to enhance light absorption and electron acceptors to promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The leakage of K+ ions and the structural destruction of the cell membrane cause bacteria death. Free radical trapping experiment proves that h+ plays a critical role on bacteria inactivation. The possible photocatalytic disinfection mechanism is also proposed. This work sheds light on designing highly-efficient photocatalysts toward water disinfection.

A novel 0D/2D CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite photocatalyst with high efficiency for water disinfection is prepared via a facile hydrothermal method.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial pollution has brought huge challenges to human health. For the moment, most human diseases are caused by drinking water infected by pathogens [1,2]. Therefore, it is urgent to seek efficient and stable water disinfection technology to reduce pathogenic microorganism pollution. The traditional methods like UV irradiation [3,4], ozonation [5], and chlorination [6] cannot be practically applied to water disinfection for their high cost, low efficiency and easily secondary contamination. Photocatalytic technology is viewed as an effective and promising method to solve this problem because it is low cost and environmentally friendly. In 1985, Matsunaga et al. firstly reported titanium dioxide (TiO2) exhibited photocatalytic activity for potential inactivation of bacteria [7]. TiO2 only responds to ultraviolet region, which leads to low utilization of sunlight and limits their wide applications [8–10]. Therefore, choosing suitable photocatalyst is essential to improve the utilization efficiency of solar energy.

Bismuth oxyhalide (BiOX, X ​= ​Cl, Br, I) has been widely reported as an excellent photocatalyst due to its unique layered structure. BiOX compounds consist of [Bi2O2] slabs interleaved with double halogen atoms slabs [11,12]. The internal electrostatic field generated by the interaction between the positive [Bi2O2]2+ slabs and negative halogen ion slabs facilitates the separation of photogenerated carriers. Bismuth-rich Bi7O9I3 contains higher contents of Bi and O, which can cause p-state anisotropy or sp hybridization, as well as high dispersion of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB). It is conducive to carrier migration and the improvement of photocatalytic activity [13,14]. However, Bi7O9I3 exhibits defects of low quantum yield and high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers [15,16]. Therefore, surface modification holds the promise to promote the separation of charge carriers.

It is reported that carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of zero dimension (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 ​nm and with good aqueous solubility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness and excellent biocompatibility, can be employed in numerous fields, such as biosensing, bioimaging, energy conversion and storage devices and so on [17–19]. According to previous studies, CQDs were also regarded as ideal dopants for improving photocatalytic activity by enhancing separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and visible light absorption. For example, Zhang and his colleagues prepared CQDs/CdS nanosheets by solvothermal method, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic efficiency of 94% for Cr(VI) reduction under saline conditions [20]. Zhao and co-workers found that modification of CQDs on g-C3N4 nanotubes showed high photocatalytic efficiency for carbamazepine (CBZ) degradation under visible light irradiation [21]. Other CQDs-based composite catalysts like CQDs/Bi2WO6 [22,23], CQDs/Bi5O7I [24] and CQDs/BiVO4 [25] were also studied. Based on the above analysis, it is possible to construct CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite catalysts by embedding 0D CQDs in 2D Bi7O9I3 microsheets, which facilitates the migration of photogenerated electrons from Bi7O9I3 to CQDs, thereby improving photocatalytic activity.

In this work, 0D CQDs-modified 2D Bi7O9I3 microsheets via a facile hydrothermal process was prepared. The composition, morphologies, microstructures, optical and photoelectrical properties of the prepared catalysts are studied in details. For the sake of evaluate its photocatalytic activity, photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), as well as degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and Rhodamine B (RhB) were carried out under visible light. The process of bacterial death was analyzed and the mechanism of photocatalytic disinfection was discussed. This work provides an enlightenment for the further research and application of Bi7O9I3 in the field of photocatalytic disinfection.

CQDs solution was obtained according to reported method with minor modification [26]. In short, citric acid (1.0507 ​g) and ethylenediamine (335 ​μL) were added into 10 ​mL deionized water. Subsequently, the clear solution was put into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for solvothermal treatment at 200 ​°C for 5 ​h. After the reaction, the brown-black product was subjected to dialysis for one day to obtain CQDs (1 ​mg/mL).

Typically, Bi7O9I3 was synthesized as follows. Firstly, 5 ​mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 50 ​ml ethylene glycol (EG) under stirred at 40 ​°C. Then, 10 ​mmol KI was slowly added in the above solution. After being stirred for 30 ​min, 1.0 ​mol/L NaOH was used to adjust the pH to 10. The mixture was kept stirring for 1 ​h. The reactant was collected by centrifugation and then rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, respectively. The yellow product was obtained and dried in an oven at 60 ​°C overnight.

CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts were obtained via hydrothermal route. 100 ​mg Bi7O9I3 was dispersed in 20 ​mL deionized water under sonication. Then, a certain amount of CQDs (0.5, 1, 2 ​mL) was added. After being stirred and ultrasonicated for 1 ​h respectively, the above mixture was put into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 140 ​°C for 4 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the powders were filtered and then rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, respectively. Finally, the samples were dried at 60 ​°C overnight, which were marked as 1-CB, 2-CB, and 3-CB, corresponding to the addition of 0.5, 1, 2 ​mL CQDs respectively. Pure Bi7O9I3 was synthesized by the same procedure without CQDs.
The relevant characterization and experimental sections can be found in the supplementary information.

E. coli and S. aureus were utilized to evaluate photocatalytic antibacterial activity of the catalysts. Firstly, 15 ​mg photocatalyst was added into 30 ​mL solution containing 3.6 ​× ​106 ​cfu/mL bacterial. Then, the antibacterial experiment was carried out under a 400 ​W metal halide lamp with a 420 ​nm cut-off filter. Finally, within a given time interval, plate colony counting method was utilized to obtain the number of colonies. Simultaneously, blank control (without photocatalyst) and dark control (without illumination) experiments were also conducted for comparison. The antibacterial rates of various catalysts were calculated as follows: antibacterial rate η = (C0–C)/C0, where C0 is the average number of colonies in blank control and C is the average number of colonies in experiment group, respectively. All experiments were carried out in triplicate and the average number of colonies was calculated.

The crystal structures and phase compositions of all the synthesized powders were characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks of Bi7O9I3 at 28.7°, 31.5°, 36.8°, 45.2°, 49.2° and 54.6° are quite similar with the typical diffraction peaks of tetragonal BiOI (JCPDS NO. 73–2062). Apparently, compared with standard peaks, the peak positions shift to smaller diffraction angle, which is attributed to the existence of rich bismuth and iodine deficiency, leading to disordered growth of the crystal [13,27,28]. The characteristic peaks of 1-CB, 2-CB and 3-CB are consistent with the pure Bi7O9I3, manifesting that the modification of CQDs shows not obvious effect on the crystal structure. No peak of CQDs is observed in the CQDs/Bi7O9I3 catalysts, which is ascribed to the low contents of CQDs.

The overall morphologies of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB were examined via scanning electron microscopy. In Fig. 2 a, pure Bi7O9I3 presents a smooth 2D microsheets structure. After modification with CQDs, the morphology of 2-CB does not change significantly (Fig. 2b). EDX spectrum (Fig. 2c) presents the characteristic signals of four elements (Bi, O, I and C), and the corresponding atomic ratio of Bi (13.72%) to I (6.17%) is 2.22 (Table S1), which is consistent with the ideal Bi7O9I3 [29,30]. Moreover, EDX mappings show that Bi, O, I and C are uniformly distributed in the selected area of 2-CB photocatalyst (Fig. 2d–i), indicating that CQDs were successfully deposited on the surface of Bi7O9I3 microsheets.

The microstructures of CQDs and 2-CB are further observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a, CQDs exhibit spherical particles with size of about 3–4 ​nm. From Fig. 3b, some black dots can be detected in 2-CB photocatalyst, which are ascribed to the existence of CQDs. From the HRTEM image (Fig. 3c), the lattice fringes with spacings of 0.301 and 0.321 ​nm can be measured, which are attributed to the (102) planes of Bi7O9I3 and (002) planes of CQDs, respectively. The above result proves the successful preparation of the CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts [31].

The existence of CQDs in CB composite photocatalysts was further investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. In Fig. 4 , the characteristic signal at 570 ​cm-1 is ascribed to the Bi–O stretching mode [13,27]. The O–H bending vibration at 3450 ​cm-1 and O–H stretching vibration at 1623 ​cm- 1 also appear, owing to the adsorption of free water molecule on the catalyst surface [13,30,32]. However, for CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalyst, weak absorption at 2923 ​cm-1 of C–H is detected, indicating the existence of CQDs [24,31]. It is worth noting that the spectrum in the low-frequency vibration region of 500–1000 ​cm-1 changes dramatically after the introduction of CQDs, manifesting that there exists an interface interaction between CQDs and Bi7O9I3.

The surface chemical states and compositions of CQDs, Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB were analyzed by XPS. The full spectra of the materials are shown in Fig. 5 a and the high-resolution XPS spectra are further studied and fitted. In Fig. S1, N 1s spectrum shows that the peak centered at 399.7 ​eV is ascribed to N–H, which is the characteristics of CQDs [26,33]. For Bi 4f spectra (Fig. 5b), two characteristic signals at 164.4 and 159.1 ​eV are associated to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively, which are the characteristic of Bi3+ species. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 5c) can be fitted into three peaks at 529.8, 531.2, 532.2 ​eV. Two peaks at 529.8, 531.2 ​eV are attributed to Bi–O and I–O, respectively, while the peak at 532.2 ​eV mainly comes from the adsorbed H2O [34]. Similarly, the I 3p spectra (Fig. 5d) shows two strong signals at 619 and 630.5 ​eV, which are corresponding to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 5e) can be fitted into three peaks at 284.6, 285.7 and 288.4 ​eV, which are assigned to C–C bond with sp2 orbital, oxygenated carbon and CO, respectively [26,31]. XPS analysis further demonstrates the successful preparation of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts.

E. coli and S. aureus were used as target bacteria to evaluate photocatalytic inactivation performance of the as-prepared samples under visible light. From Fig. S2 and Fig. 6 a, E. coli inactivation does not happen in the absence of catalysts, indicating that E. coli can grow and reproduce normally under visible light irradiation only. Moreover, the addition of catalysts also plays negligible effect on the inactivation of bacteria in the dark, which demonstrates that the prepared catalysts possess low cytotoxicity. Therefore, photocatalytic inactivation efficiency of CQDs, Bi7O9I3, 1-CB, 2-CB and 3-CB are investigated under visible light. The inactivation efficiencies against E. coli over CQDs, Bi7O9I3, 1-CB, 2-CB and 3-CB are 5.14, 36.29, 93.47, 99.99 and 96.55%, respectively. Both pure CQDs and Bi7O9I3 exhibit poor antibacterial properties. With the modification of CQDs on Bi7O9I3, the photocatalytic antibacterial activity of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 is significantly enhanced. Among them, 2-CB presents the highest antibacterial performance. However, upon increasing the loading levels of CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of the composite catalyst (3-CB) decreases. The excessive CQDs loading on Bi7O9I3 surface restrict effective utilization of the incident photons, causing poor light exposure of Bi7O9I3 for photogeneration of charge carriers [25].

The antibacterial result is analyzed by the pseudo first-order kinetic model and the equation is ln (C/C0) ​= ​-kt. In Fig. 6b, 2-CB exhibits the largest k value (0.08811 min-1), which is about 19.7 times higher than pure Bi7O9I3 (0.00447 min-1). The result reveals that a certain amount of CQDs modification facilitates the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers, which is beneficial to improve the photocatalytic activity. Recycling experiment was carried out to study the stability of 2-CB photocatalyst towards E. coli inactivation. In Fig. S3, 2-CB shows slight decrease of bacteria inactivation efficiency, indicating that the 2-CB photocatalyst maintains high stability.

Different from E. coli, S. aureus is a typical representative of Gram-positive bacteria. The inactivation of the photocatalysts towards S. aureus is also studied. After modification with CQDs, the CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts show high photocatalytic activity against S. aureus. As indicated in Figs. S4a–b, 2-CB displays the highest photocatalytic antibacterial activity (99.99%) and the largest k (0.08916 min-1). It can consolidate the conclusion that 2-CB photocatalyst exhibits optimum photocatalytic activity to remove pathogenic bacteria from water.

To explore application of the catalysts for photocatalytic degradation, ciprofloxacin and rhodamine B are used as target pollutants to study the photocatalytic degradation performance. In Figs. S5a–b, the highest photocatalytic efficiency for ciprofloxacin and rhodamine B degradation over 2-CB is up to 80.28% and 97.45%, respectively. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was investigated in different pH solutions (Figs. S5c–d). The degradation efficiency for CIP and RhB is the highest at pH of 7.0 and 5.0, respectively. It may be ascribed to the highest adsorption of pollutants on the surface of the catalyst at the pH values [35,36]. In summary, the modification of CQDs on Bi7O9I3 is an effective strategy for improving photocatalytic activity, which can meet the practical water disinfection.

Zeta potentials are examined to study the formation process of 2-CB composite photocatalyst, and the interaction between the as-prepared samples and bacteria as well. In Fig. S6, the zeta potentials of CQDs and Bi7O9I3 are −5.948 and 37.32 ​mV, respectively, which is very beneficial for the electrostatic adsorption of CQDs on the surface of Bi7O9I3. The zeta potential of 2-CB is 29.94 ​mV, while the zeta potentials of E. coli and S. aureus are −6.876 and −4.398 ​mV, respectively. The positively charged 2-CB is easily anchored to the negatively charged bacterial by electrostatic force, thus exhibiting excellent photocatalytic antibacterial activity.

As one of the most abundant cations in cells, K+ can promote the activity of intracellular enzymes [37]. Therefore, in the bacterial inactivation process, the change of cell membrane permeability is measured by monitoring the leakage of K+. In Fig. S7, with the prolonged illumination time, the concentration of K+ leaked from the bacterial cells increases significantly. It can be concluded that the continuous leakage of K+, the gradual destruction of cell membranes and the disclosure of cell contents result in the death of bacterial.

Fluorescence microscope was used to observe cellular survive-death mode by using fluorescent nucleic acid probes SYTO9 and PI to stain E. coli DNA. SYTO9 can mark both live and dead cells (green), while PI only marks dead cells (red) with damaged cell membranes [37–39]. Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence images of E. coli treated with 2-CB photocatalysis for different time. Live bacteria are dominant before the photocatalytic reaction (0 ​min). The numbers of dead bacteria with red fluorescence gradually increase with the prolonged reaction time. Therefore, Fluorescence staining observation further proves that the destruction of bacterial cell membrane structure and genetic material such as DNA are possible ways for bacterial death.

In order to understand the cause of bacterial cell death under visible light irradiation, SEM images of E. coli are recorded. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the E. coli cells are normal and intact before photocatalytic treatment, with a smooth surface and a rod-like morphology. After photocatalytic treatment for 90 ​min (Fig. 8b), it is found that the catalyst particles are firmly adsorbed on the surface of E. coli, which is consistent with the result of Zeta potential analysis. Furthermore, the cell membrane is sunken, and the cell structure is severely deformed and destroyed. According to previous reports, after the cell membrane is destroyed, the DNA and other genetic material will be leaked and destroyed, which will cause the death of E. coli [40,41].

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded to analyze optical properties of the as-prepared catalysts. As shown in Fig. 9 a, pure Bi7O9I3 exhibits light absorption ranging from 200 to 514 ​nm. For CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts, CQDs act as a photosensitizer with excellent light harvest ability. Therefore, upon increasing CQDs contents, the light harvesting capability of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts within the visible light region enhanced. Moreover, Kubelka-Munk transformed reflectance spectra are used to calculate bandgap energy (Eg) of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB (Fig. 9b). The Eg values of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB are 2.41 and 2.33 ​eV, respectively. Valence band XPS spectra are measured to obtain valence band (VB) values of the prepared samples. In Fig. 9c, Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB exhibit a similar VB value of 1.67 ​eV, which demonstrates that the electronic structure of Bi7O9I3 matrix does not changed with modification of CQDs. Therefore, according to the formula of ECB ​= ​EVB - Eg, the CB values of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB are calculated to be −0.74 and −0.66 ​eV, where EVB, Eg and ECB are VB potential, bandgap energy and CB potential, respectively. The energy level distributions of CB and VB are summarized and illustrated in Fig. 9d.

PL emission spectra of Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB are displayed in Fig. S8. Compared to that of Bi7O9I3, the peak intensity of 2-CB decreases, indicating that the existence of CQDs on the surface of Bi7O9I3 effectively limits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Photoelectrochemical property was also investigated to evaluate the charge separation efficiency. It can be seen in Fig. 10 a that the photocurrent density of 2-CB is larger than that of pure Bi7O9I3, indicating that 2-CB holds higher charge separation efficiency under the intermittent illumination of visible light. In addition, the transient photocurrent responses of the catalysts can almost maintain a stable value after five on/off irradiation cycles, revealing high photoelectrochemical stability of the catalysts. The arc radius is related to the charge transfer resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface. EIS plots of the corresponding samples are shown in Fig. 10b. The smaller arc radius order of 2-CB reveals lower charge transfer resistance and better interface charge transfer capability. Therefore, photoelectrochemical study further reveals the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity.

2-CB catalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic performance towards bacterial inactivation, which could be attributed to the production of free radicals in the photocatalytic process. Hence, radical trapping experiments are conducted to study the roles of main species such as ∙O2 -, h+ and ∙OH on the inactivation of E. coli. TEMPOL, EDTA-2Na and IPA are used to trap ∙O2 -, h+ and ∙OH, respectively. In Fig. S9, scavengers show negligible toxic effect on E. coli. As shown in Fig. 11 , the bacterial inactivation efficiency is significantly inhibited upon addition of EDTA-2Na, which indicates that holes are the main species in the photocatalytic process. However, the photocatalytic inactivation efficiency is slightly affected after addition of TEMPOL or IPA, indicating that ∙O2 - and ∙OH are not the main active species towards inactivation of bacteria.

EPR spectra were recorded to detect ∙OH and ∙O2 - with DMPO as a trapping agent in aqueous solution and methanol solution, respectively. In Fig. S10a, no ∙OH signal is detected for both Bi7O9I3 and 2-CB, indicating that the photoinduced h+ (1.67 ​eV, NHE) cannot oxidize the adsorbed H2O to ∙OH (1.99 ​eV, NHE). The signal for 2-CB after light irradiation for 5 ​min is detected and the signals marked corresponding to ∙O2 -. As shown in Fig. S10b, the signal of ∙O2 - is detected under visible light irradiation, and the main peak of 2-CB is significantly higher than the main peak of Bi7O9I3. It can be concluded that modification of CQDs is helpful to the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, thereby improving the utilization efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes.

The mechanism of photocatalytic disinfection is tentatively proposed, which is schematically illustrated in Fig. 12 . The CQDs are anchored on the surface of the Bi7O9I3 microsheets because of electrostatic adsorption. The photogenerated electrons are excited from VB to CB of Bi7O9I3 under the excitation of visible light, and then transfer to CQDs. The electron reacts with O2 to generate active species ∙O2 -, while the photogenerated holes cannot oxidize H2O to generate ∙OH. Therefore, it is favorable for the photogenerated holes to directly attack bacteria or pollutants. The mechanism can be concluded as follows: on one hand, CQDs can be used as photosensitizers to enhance light absorption. On the other hand, CQDs act as electron accepters, which promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers.

In summary, a novel 0D/2D CQDs/Bi7O9I3 composite is constructed via a facile hydrothermal process. Compared with pure Bi7O9I3 microsheets, the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/Bi7O9I3 photocatalysts for inactivating bacteria and removing organic pollutants are significantly enhanced under visible light irradiation. The optimized catalyst 2-CB possesses the highest photocatalytic activity and high stability. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is mainly ascribed to the enhancement of light absorption and more efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers upon modification of CQDs. This study also reveals that h+ is the main active species towards inactivation of bacteria, causing leakage of K+, cell membrane destruction, and bacterial death. This work provides not only feasibility of CQDs/Bi7O9I3 as a promising bacterial antibiotic, but also new insights for designing composite photocatalysts towards water disinfection.

Qing Zhong: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei-De Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition. Qingshan Shi: Supervision, Resources. Yu-Xiang Yu: Discussion, References.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21773074).

Science
